_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against the 2023 International Evidence-based PCOS Guideline (PMID 37580314), Azziz 2016 Nat Rev Dis Primers (PMID 27510637), and Teede 2018 Hum Reprod. Diagnostic criteria reflect the 2023 update including the AMH option.

h1Title: PCOS: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is a hormonal and metabolic disorder in which the ovaries produce excess androgens and ovulation becomes irregular or absent. It is the most common endocrine disorder in women of reproductive age, affecting an estimated 8-13% depending on the diagnostic criteria used, and as many as 70% of affected women are undiagnosed. The condition typically shows up in the late teens or twenties as irregular periods, acne, excess facial or body hair, and difficulty losing weight. Beyond fertility, PCOS carries lifelong metabolic consequences — insulin resistance, a high risk of type 2 diabetes, and elevated cardiovascular risk. There is no cure, but symptoms and long-term risks are very manageable with lifestyle measures, ovulation-targeted medication, and treatments aimed at the specific concern, whether that is irregular cycles, unwanted hair, or fertility.

keyStats.prevalence: 8-13% of reproductive-age women depending on criteria; up to 70% undiagnosed (2023 International PCOS Guideline)
keyStats.demographics: Onset typically in adolescence; affects women across all ethnicities, with higher metabolic risk reported in South Asian women
keyStats.avgAge: Most often diagnosed between ages 18 and 35; symptoms usually begin around the time of puberty
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated over 100 million women worldwide affected

definition: Polycystic ovary syndrome (ICD-10: E28.2) is a heterogeneous endocrine-metabolic disorder defined by a combination of androgen excess, ovulatory dysfunction, and polycystic ovarian morphology. Diagnosis in adults uses the Rotterdam criteria as adopted by the 2023 International Evidence-based Guideline: at least two of three features — clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, irregular or absent ovulation, and polycystic ovaries on ultrasound — with other causes excluded. The 2023 update allows an elevated anti-Mullerian hormone (AMH) level to substitute for ultrasound in adults. The underlying biology centers on two interacting drivers: increased ovarian and adrenal androgen production, and insulin resistance with compensatory high insulin levels, which itself further stimulates ovarian androgen output and suppresses sex hormone-binding globulin. The result is disrupted follicle development, so eggs mature but do not reliably release, producing irregular cycles and the multiple small follicles ('polycystic' appearance) that give the syndrome its name — though these are arrested follicles, not true cysts. PCOS is not solely a gynecologic condition; it is a systemic disorder spanning reproductive, metabolic, dermatologic, and psychological domains. It is managed by endocrinologists, gynecologists, and primary care physicians, often together, with reproductive specialists involved when fertility is the priority.

typesClassification.description: Hyperandrogenism plus ovulatory dysfunction plus polycystic ovaries — all three features. This is the most common phenotype in specialist clinics and carries the highest metabolic risk.
Hyperandrogenism plus ovulatory dysfunction, without polycystic ovarian morphology. Metabolic risk is similar to phenotype A.
Hyperandrogenism plus polycystic ovaries, with preserved ovulation. Periods may be regular; metabolic risk is intermediate.
Ovulatory dysfunction plus polycystic ovaries, without androgen excess. The mildest metabolic phenotype, and the one most often debated diagnostically.

primarySymptoms: Irregular, infrequent, or absent menstrual periods — typically cycles longer than 35 days or fewer than eight periods a year — reflecting failure to ovulate regularly.
Excess facial and body hair (hirsutism) in a male pattern, on the upper lip, chin, chest, or abdomen, caused by elevated androgens.
Acne that persists beyond adolescence or appears along the jawline and lower face, often resistant to standard topical treatment.
Difficulty losing weight and a tendency to gain weight around the abdomen, linked to insulin resistance.
Thinning hair or male-pattern hair loss at the crown and temples (androgenic alopecia).
Difficulty becoming pregnant, because ovulation is irregular or absent — PCOS is the leading cause of anovulatory infertility.
Darkened, velvety patches of skin (acanthosis nigricans) in the neck folds, armpits, or groin, a visible marker of insulin resistance.
Skin tags, particularly around the neck and armpits, another sign associated with insulin resistance.
Mood symptoms including anxiety and depression, which occur at higher rates in PCOS than in the general population.
Heavy or prolonged bleeding when periods do occur, because the uterine lining builds up over long anovulatory intervals.
Recurrent pregnancy loss in some women, related to hormonal and metabolic disturbance.
Sleep disturbance and daytime fatigue, with obstructive sleep apnea more common in PCOS independent of weight.
earlyWarningSigns: Periods that never settle into a regular pattern after the first few years following menarche
New or worsening acne and excess hair growth in the late teens or early twenties
Weight gain concentrated around the waist, with difficulty losing it despite usual efforts
A first-degree relative with PCOS, type 2 diabetes, or early-onset metabolic disease
Darkening of skin in the neck creases or armpits, an early sign of insulin resistance
emergencySigns: Very heavy or prolonged vaginal bleeding causing dizziness, weakness, or fainting — possible significant blood loss requiring urgent assessment
Sudden, rapidly progressing excess hair growth, voice deepening, or clitoral enlargement — raises concern for an androgen-secreting tumor rather than PCOS
Severe pelvic pain with nausea and vomiting — possible ovarian torsion, particularly relevant if fertility treatment has enlarged the ovaries
Symptoms of a blood clot — calf swelling and pain, or sudden breathlessness and chest pain — in a woman taking combined hormonal contraception for PCOS
Signs of severely uncontrolled blood sugar — extreme thirst, frequent urination, blurred vision, and weight loss — in a woman with PCOS who has developed diabetes

causes.description: Many women with PCOS have tissues that respond poorly to insulin, so the pancreas releases more of it. High insulin directly stimulates the ovaries to make more androgens and lowers sex hormone-binding globulin, raising free testosterone. This is a central driver of both the reproductive and metabolic features.

causes.cause: Excess ovarian and adrenal androgen production
causes.description: The theca cells of the ovary, and to a lesser degree the adrenal glands, produce above-normal amounts of androgens in PCOS. This androgen excess disrupts the maturation and release of egg-containing follicles and produces hirsutism, acne, and scalp hair loss.

The hypothalamus releases gonadotropin-releasing hormone at a faster pulse frequency, biasing the pituitary toward luteinizing hormone over follicle-stimulating hormone. The resulting high LH-to-FSH ratio further drives ovarian androgen output and impairs follicle selection and ovulation.

PCOS clusters strongly in families. Twin studies estimate heritability above 70%, and genome-wide studies have identified variants in genes affecting gonadotropin action, insulin signaling, and follicle development. A daughter or sister of a woman with PCOS has a substantially increased risk.

Obesity does not cause PCOS but worsens it: excess fat tissue increases insulin resistance, raises inflammatory signaling, and converts hormones in ways that intensify androgen excess and anovulation. This is why even modest weight loss improves symptoms in women with overweight.

Evidence suggests that androgen exposure during fetal development, and possibly low birth weight followed by rapid catch-up growth, can program a PCOS-prone phenotype. This helps explain why the syndrome often becomes apparent right at puberty.

riskFactors.description: Having a mother or sister with PCOS markedly raises personal risk; twin studies estimate heritability above 70%, reflecting inherited variation in insulin and gonadotropin signaling.
PCOS and type 2 diabetes share insulin-resistance genetics; a family history of diabetes increases both the likelihood of PCOS and the metabolic severity of the syndrome.
Excess adiposity worsens insulin resistance and androgen excess and intensifies anovulation. It does not cause PCOS but amplifies every feature, and weight loss measurably improves symptoms.
Impaired insulin sensitivity is present in a large share of women with PCOS regardless of weight and directly drives ovarian androgen production and cycle disruption.
Low physical activity worsens insulin resistance and weight gain, compounding the metabolic features of PCOS; regular activity improves insulin sensitivity independent of weight loss.
Women of South Asian background tend to develop PCOS at lower body weights and with more pronounced insulin resistance and metabolic risk than some other populations.
Early appearance of pubic hair and adrenal androgen activity in childhood is associated with a higher chance of developing PCOS at puberty.

riskFactors.factor: Low birth weight with rapid catch-up growth
riskFactors.description: Being born small and then gaining weight rapidly in early childhood is linked to later insulin resistance and a PCOS-prone metabolic pattern.
Valproate, used for epilepsy and bipolar disorder, is associated with weight gain, hyperandrogenism, and a higher rate of PCOS-like features in some women.

affectedDemographics: Affects an estimated 8-13% of reproductive-age women, with up to 70% undiagnosed worldwide (2023 International PCOS Guideline)
Symptoms typically begin around puberty; most diagnoses are made between ages 18 and 35
Occurs across all ethnic groups; South Asian women tend to present with more severe insulin resistance at lower body weights
PCOS is the leading cause of anovulatory infertility, accounting for the majority of cases
Women with PCOS have roughly a 4-fold higher risk of developing type 2 diabetes compared with women without the syndrome
diagnosisOverview: PCOS is a clinical diagnosis of exclusion — there is no single confirmatory test. The 2023 International Evidence-based Guideline uses the Rotterdam criteria for adults: a diagnosis requires at least two of three features — clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, ovulatory dysfunction, and polycystic ovarian morphology — after other conditions that mimic PCOS have been ruled out. The assessment starts with a careful menstrual history and an examination for hirsutism, acne, and acanthosis nigricans. Blood tests measure total and free testosterone and other androgens to document hyperandrogenism, alongside tests that exclude mimics: thyroid function, prolactin, and 17-hydroxyprogesterone to rule out non-classic congenital adrenal hyperplasia, with additional testing if Cushing's syndrome or an androgen-secreting tumor is suspected by rapid or severe symptoms. Pelvic ultrasound assesses ovarian morphology, looking for a high follicle count or increased ovarian volume; the 2023 guideline allows an elevated anti-Mullerian hormone level to substitute for ultrasound in adults. Importantly, ultrasound is not used to diagnose PCOS in adolescents within eight years of their first period, because multifollicular ovaries are normal in that age group — adolescent diagnosis relies instead on hyperandrogenism plus persistent menstrual irregularity. Once PCOS is confirmed, the workup shifts to risk stratification: every woman should be screened for impaired glucose tolerance with an oral glucose tolerance test or HbA1c, have lipids and blood pressure checked, and be screened for depression, anxiety, and obstructive sleep apnea, since these conditions are substantially more common in PCOS and shape long-term management.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum total and free testosterone (and androstenedione, DHEAS)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Documents biochemical hyperandrogenism, one of the three diagnostic criteria. Very high or rapidly rising levels prompt a search for an androgen-secreting tumor instead of PCOS.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A morning blood draw, ideally in the early follicular phase if cycling. No special preparation. Results return within a day or two.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses ovarian morphology — follicle number and ovarian volume — to identify polycystic ovaries. Not used for diagnosis in adolescents within 8 years of menarche.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Either an abdominal scan with a full bladder or a transvaginal scan. Painless, takes 15-20 minutes, no radiation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reflects the number of small ovarian follicles and, under the 2023 guideline, can substitute for ultrasound in diagnosing polycystic ovarian morphology in adults.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single blood test that can be drawn at any point in the cycle. Results typically available within a few days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes the main conditions that mimic PCOS — thyroid disease, hyperprolactinemia, and non-classic congenital adrenal hyperplasia — which is essential because PCOS is a diagnosis of exclusion.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A routine blood draw, often combined with the androgen tests. The 17-hydroxyprogesterone sample is best taken in the morning.

diagnosticTests.test: Oral glucose tolerance test or HbA1c
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for impaired glucose tolerance and type 2 diabetes, which are substantially more common in PCOS. Recommended at diagnosis and periodically thereafter.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: For the glucose tolerance test, a fasting blood sample, a sweet glucose drink, then a repeat sample two hours later. HbA1c is a single non-fasting draw.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses cardiovascular risk, which is elevated in PCOS. Forms part of the metabolic screening recommended for every woman at diagnosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw, fasting or non-fasting per local practice, plus a standard blood pressure measurement at the clinic visit.

treatmentOverview: PCOS has no cure, so treatment is tailored to the woman's current priorities — cycle regulation, managing hyperandrogenism, fertility, or metabolic risk — and reassessed as those priorities change. The 2023 International Evidence-based Guideline places lifestyle measures at the foundation of care for all women with PCOS: a sustainable, individualized eating pattern combined with regular physical activity. In women with overweight, a weight loss of just 5-10% can restore ovulation, lower androgen levels, and improve insulin sensitivity. For a woman not seeking pregnancy, combined hormonal contraception (the pill, patch, or ring) is first-line: it regulates cycles, protects the uterine lining from the unopposed estrogen exposure that raises endometrial cancer risk, and reduces acne and hirsutism over 6-9 months. Metformin is added or used alone primarily for metabolic benefit and is particularly useful in women with impaired glucose tolerance or a high diabetes risk; it modestly improves menstrual regularity and weight. For hirsutism that does not respond adequately to combined contraception, an anti-androgen such as spironolactone is added, alongside cosmetic measures and topical eflornithine for facial hair. When pregnancy is the goal, the guideline names letrozole as the first-line ovulation induction agent — it achieves higher live birth rates than clomiphene citrate in women with PCOS — with metformin as an adjunct and gonadotropins or ovarian drilling as second-line options. In vitro fertilization is effective when first-line ovulation induction fails. Newer agents, including GLP-1 receptor agonists, are increasingly used for weight and metabolic management in PCOS, though they must be stopped before conception. Mental health is treated as a core component of care, not an afterthought, given the high rates of anxiety and depression. The overarching principle is long-term, individualized management with regular metabolic and cardiovascular screening, because the consequences of PCOS extend well beyond the reproductive years.

medicalTreatments.name: Combined hormonal contraception (estrogen-progestin pill, patch, or ring)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line for women not seeking pregnancy. Regulates menstrual cycles, protects the endometrium from unopposed estrogen, and reduces acne and hirsutism by lowering free androgens. Choice of formulation is individualized; no single pill is clearly superior.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Restores predictable withdrawal bleeds in over 90% of users and reduces hirsutism scores by roughly 15-25% over 6-9 months of use.

medicalTreatments.name: Metformin (commonly 1500-2000 mg daily in divided doses)
medicalTreatments.description: An insulin-sensitizing drug used for metabolic benefit, especially with impaired glucose tolerance or high diabetes risk. Also modestly improves menstrual regularity and supports weight management. Started low and titrated up to limit gastrointestinal side effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves menstrual frequency and ovulation in roughly 30-50% of women and reduces progression to type 2 diabetes; weight effect is modest, typically 1-3 kg.

medicalTreatments.name: Spironolactone (50-100 mg daily) and other anti-androgens
medicalTreatments.description: Added for hirsutism or androgenic hair loss that does not respond adequately to combined contraception. Blocks androgen receptors and reduces androgen production. Must be combined with reliable contraception because it can affect a male fetus.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces hirsutism in roughly 60-70% of women when used for at least 6 months, often in combination with a contraceptive pill.

medicalTreatments.name: Letrozole (2.5-7.5 mg daily for 5 days each cycle)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line ovulation induction agent for women with PCOS seeking pregnancy, recommended ahead of clomiphene by the 2023 guideline. An aromatase inhibitor that stimulates follicle development. Used under monitoring to limit the risk of multiple pregnancy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves ovulation in roughly 60-80% of women and produces higher live birth rates than clomiphene citrate in PCOS.

medicalTreatments.name: Clomiphene citrate (50-150 mg daily for 5 days each cycle)
medicalTreatments.description: An alternative ovulation induction agent, used when letrozole is unavailable or not tolerated. Often combined with metformin to improve ovulation rates.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Induces ovulation in roughly 60-70% of women, with cumulative live birth rates somewhat lower than letrozole in head-to-head trials.

medicalTreatments.name: GLP-1 receptor agonists (for example liraglutide, semaglutide)
medicalTreatments.description: Increasingly used for weight and metabolic management in women with PCOS and overweight or obesity. Reduce appetite and body weight and improve insulin sensitivity. Must be stopped before attempting conception.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Produce average weight reductions of roughly 5-15% depending on agent and dose, with associated improvements in androgen levels and cycle regularity.

medicalTreatments.name: Gonadotropin therapy or laparoscopic ovarian drilling
medicalTreatments.description: Second-line fertility treatments when oral ovulation induction fails. Injectable gonadotropins stimulate follicle growth under close monitoring; ovarian drilling uses small ovarian punctures to lower androgen output and restore ovulation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Gonadotropins induce ovulation in over 70% of women; ovarian drilling restores ovulation in roughly 50-80%, with the advantage of avoiding multiple pregnancy.

surgicalOptions.name: Laparoscopic ovarian drilling
surgicalOptions.description: A keyhole procedure that creates several small punctures in the ovarian surface using heat or laser, reducing androgen-producing tissue and restoring the hormonal balance needed for ovulation. Reserved as a second-line fertility option when oral agents fail, or when laparoscopy is being done for another reason.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Restores spontaneous ovulation in roughly 50-80% of selected women, with live birth rates comparable to gonadotropin therapy and a lower risk of multiple pregnancy.

surgicalOptions.name: Bariatric (metabolic) surgery
surgicalOptions.description: Considered for women with PCOS and severe obesity when lifestyle and medication have not achieved adequate weight loss. Substantial weight reduction frequently restores ovulation and resolves many metabolic features, though pregnancy is generally delayed 12-18 months after surgery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Produces sustained weight loss of 25-30% in most patients, with restoration of regular cycles in a large majority of previously anovulatory women.

alternativeTreatments.name: Inositol (myo-inositol, often with D-chiro-inositol)
alternativeTreatments.description: A nutritional supplement that acts as an insulin sensitizer. Several randomized trials report modest improvements in ovulation, menstrual regularity, and metabolic markers in PCOS, though the 2023 guideline notes the evidence is limited and inositol should not replace established treatments. It is generally well tolerated.

alternativeTreatments.name: Structured exercise programs
alternativeTreatments.description: Both aerobic and resistance training improve insulin sensitivity, body composition, and cardiometabolic markers in PCOS, with some benefit to ovulation, even without significant weight loss. Exercise is an evidence-based core component of management rather than an optional add-on.

whySeeSpecialist: An endocrinologist or gynecologist should be involved when the diagnosis is uncertain, when symptoms are severe or rapidly progressing (which can signal a different androgen disorder), when metabolic complications such as diabetes develop, or when standard treatments are not controlling symptoms. A reproductive medicine specialist should manage fertility when ovulation induction is needed, particularly if first-line treatment fails. Adolescents with suspected PCOS benefit from specialist assessment because diagnostic criteria differ in that age group.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a clinician — endocrinologist, gynecologist, or experienced primary care physician — who manages PCOS as a lifelong metabolic condition, not just irregular periods. Ask whether they screen for diabetes, lipids, blood pressure, mental health, and sleep apnea, and whether they tailor treatment to your current priorities. For fertility, choose a reproductive specialist who uses letrozole as first-line and monitors ovulation induction carefully. Continuity matters, since PCOS care evolves across the reproductive years and beyond.
preventionStrategies: Maintain a healthy weight from adolescence onward, since excess adiposity amplifies every feature of PCOS even though it does not cause it
Build regular physical activity into the week — at least 150 minutes of moderate activity — to protect insulin sensitivity
Get screened early if you have a family history of PCOS or type 2 diabetes, so the condition is identified and managed before complications develop
Address insulin resistance promptly with lifestyle changes, and medication where indicated, to lower the risk of progressing to type 2 diabetes
Have regular metabolic checks — glucose, lipids, and blood pressure — so cardiovascular and diabetes risk is caught and treated early
lifestyleModifications: Aim for a sustainable, individualized eating pattern rather than a fad diet — the 2023 guideline finds no single 'PCOS diet' is superior, so consistency matters more than the specific plan
If you have overweight, target a gradual 5-10% weight loss, which can restore ovulation and substantially improve androgen and metabolic markers
Combine aerobic activity with resistance training, both of which improve insulin sensitivity even without weight loss
Prioritize sleep and get assessed for obstructive sleep apnea if you snore or wake unrefreshed, as it is more common in PCOS and worsens metabolic health
Treat acne and hirsutism with the medical and cosmetic options available rather than waiting them out, since they significantly affect quality of life
Seek support for anxiety, depression, or disordered eating, which occur at higher rates in PCOS and respond to treatment
Do not smoke and limit alcohol, since both add to the cardiovascular risk that PCOS already carries

recommended: A balanced eating pattern emphasizing vegetables, fruit, whole grains, legumes, and lean protein
Lower-glycemic-index carbohydrates, which produce gentler blood sugar and insulin responses
Adequate fiber to improve satiety, glucose control, and gut health
Healthy fats from nuts, seeds, oily fish, and olive oil in place of saturated and trans fats
Portion-aware, regular meals to support gradual, sustainable weight management
avoid: Frequent sugar-sweetened beverages and refined sugars, which spike insulin
Highly processed, energy-dense foods that promote weight gain and insulin resistance
Very-low-calorie crash diets, which are hard to sustain and tend to rebound
Excess alcohol, which adds empty calories and cardiovascular risk
Treating any single food as a cure — there is no evidence that eliminating dairy or gluten benefits PCOS in the absence of a specific intolerance

exerciseGuidelines: The 2023 guideline recommends at least 150 minutes per week of moderate-intensity activity, or 75 minutes of vigorous activity, for adults with PCOS, with muscle-strengthening exercise on two or more days. For weight loss or to prevent weight regain, more activity — around 250 minutes per week of moderate exercise — is advised. Exercise improves insulin sensitivity, body composition, mood, and ovulation independent of weight loss, so it is worthwhile even when the scale does not move. Choose activities that are sustainable and enjoyable to support long-term adherence.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed medication consistently — the contraceptive pill, metformin, or anti-androgen — as their benefits build over months, not days
Track menstrual cycles with an app or diary so changes in pattern are noticed and discussed
Build movement into each day, since regular activity is one of the most effective ongoing treatments
Make consistent, realistic food choices rather than alternating between strict dieting and rebound eating
Keep up scheduled metabolic screening — glucose, lipids, blood pressure — and mental health check-ins, since PCOS risks are lifelong
prognosis: PCOS is a lifelong condition, but its outlook is generally good when it is actively managed. Most women with PCOS who wish to conceive eventually can, either spontaneously or with ovulation induction, and cumulative live birth rates with first-line treatment are high. Symptoms such as hirsutism, acne, and irregular cycles respond well to medication, and many features ease after menopause as androgen levels fall, although the metabolic risks persist. The main long-term concerns are metabolic and cardiovascular: women with PCOS have roughly a 4-fold higher risk of type 2 diabetes, higher rates of hypertension and abnormal lipids, and an increased risk of endometrial cancer from years of unopposed estrogen if cycles are not managed. Anxiety and depression are also more common. None of these outcomes is inevitable — they are substantially reduced by maintaining a healthy weight, treating insulin resistance, protecting the endometrium with regular cycle regulation, and keeping up metabolic screening. The decisive prognostic factor is sustained, individualized management across the reproductive years and beyond, rather than the severity of symptoms at diagnosis.
recoveryTimeline: PCOS is not an illness that resolves, but its features respond on predictable timelines once treatment starts. Combined hormonal contraception regulates bleeding within the first one to two cycles, while its effect on acne and hirsutism builds over 6-9 months because hair and skin turnover are slow. Metformin's metabolic and menstrual effects appear over 3-6 months. With lifestyle change, a 5-10% weight loss over several months can restore ovulation. Letrozole or clomiphene typically induces ovulation within the first one to three treated cycles when it works. Mental health and metabolic improvements track with sustained lifestyle change over months to years. Management continues across the reproductive years and beyond.
complications: Type 2 diabetes — women with PCOS have roughly a 4-fold higher risk, driven by insulin resistance; detected early with regular glucose screening
Endometrial hyperplasia and endometrial cancer — years of unopposed estrogen from absent ovulation thicken the uterine lining; prevented by regular cycle regulation or progestin use
Cardiovascular disease — higher rates of hypertension, abnormal lipids, and metabolic syndrome raise long-term cardiovascular risk; monitored with periodic checks
Infertility and pregnancy complications — anovulation causes subfertility, and pregnancies carry higher rates of gestational diabetes, pre-eclampsia, and miscarriage
Obstructive sleep apnea — more common in PCOS independent of weight, worsening fatigue and metabolic health; screened for if snoring or unrefreshing sleep is present
Anxiety, depression, and disordered eating — substantially more common in PCOS and important to identify and treat as part of routine care

supportResources.name: PCOS Awareness Association
supportResources.description: US patient organization with education, community, and resources for women living with PCOS.

supportResources.name: Verity — UK PCOS Charity
supportResources.description: UK charity offering information, support, and advocacy for women with PCOS.

supportResources.name: Monash Centre for Health Research and Implementation — PCOS Guideline
supportResources.description: Home of the 2023 International Evidence-based PCOS Guideline, with patient resources and the AskPCOS app.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypothyroidism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hypothyroidism can also cause irregular periods, weight gain, fatigue, and hair changes, which is why thyroid function is tested when PCOS is suspected. Hypothyroidism is confirmed by an elevated TSH, while PCOS shows hyperandrogenism and normal thyroid tests. The two are distinguished by blood work, and both can coexist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cushing's Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cushing's syndrome shares weight gain, irregular periods, acne, and excess hair with PCOS, but adds features PCOS does not — purple stretch marks, easy bruising, a rounded face, proximal muscle weakness, and high blood pressure. Cushing's is confirmed by cortisol testing and is screened for when these distinguishing features are present.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hyperthyroidism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hyperthyroidism can also disrupt menstrual cycles, but it typically causes weight loss, heat intolerance, a fast heart rate, and anxiety, rather than the weight gain and hyperandrogenism of PCOS. A suppressed TSH with high thyroid hormone confirms hyperthyroidism and separates it from PCOS.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acromegaly
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acromegaly, caused by excess growth hormone from a pituitary tumor, can produce menstrual irregularity, skin changes, and insulin resistance that overlap with PCOS. It is distinguished by its characteristic features — enlarging hands, feet, and facial bones — and confirmed by IGF-1 testing and pituitary imaging.

relatedConditions.name: Type 2 Diabetes
relatedConditions.relevance: PCOS raises the risk of type 2 diabetes roughly 4-fold through shared insulin resistance.

relatedConditions.name: Metabolic Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: PCOS frequently overlaps with metabolic syndrome, sharing insulin resistance, central adiposity, and abnormal lipids.

relatedConditions.name: Female Infertility Associated with Anovulation
relatedConditions.relevance: PCOS is the leading cause of anovulatory infertility in women of reproductive age.

relatedConditions.name: Endometrial Hyperplasia
relatedConditions.relevance: Long anovulatory intervals expose the uterine lining to unopposed estrogen, raising the risk of hyperplasia and endometrial cancer.

relatedConditions.name: Depression
relatedConditions.relevance: Depression and anxiety occur at substantially higher rates in women with PCOS and are treated as a core part of care.

faqs.question: What is PCOS?
faqs.answer: PCOS, or polycystic ovary syndrome, is a hormonal and metabolic disorder in which the ovaries produce excess androgens and ovulation becomes irregular or absent. It is the most common endocrine disorder in women of reproductive age, affecting an estimated 8-13%. Diagnosis requires at least two of three features: hyperandrogenism, ovulatory dysfunction, and polycystic ovaries.

faqs.question: What are the main symptoms of PCOS?
faqs.answer: The main symptoms are irregular or absent periods, excess facial and body hair, persistent acne, and difficulty losing weight. Other features include scalp hair thinning, darkened skin patches, mood symptoms, and difficulty becoming pregnant. Symptoms vary widely between women, and not everyone has all of them.

faqs.question: What causes PCOS?
faqs.answer: PCOS is caused by an interaction of insulin resistance, excess ovarian and adrenal androgen production, and disordered hormone signaling from the brain, on a background of strong genetic predisposition. Excess body weight does not cause PCOS but worsens every feature. The exact trigger is not fully understood, but heritability is estimated above 70%.

faqs.question: How is PCOS diagnosed?
faqs.answer: PCOS is a diagnosis of exclusion based on the Rotterdam criteria: at least two of three features — hyperandrogenism, irregular ovulation, and polycystic ovaries on ultrasound or a high AMH level — after ruling out other causes. Tests include androgen levels, thyroid function, prolactin, and 17-hydroxyprogesterone. There is no single confirmatory test.

faqs.question: Is PCOS curable?
faqs.answer: PCOS cannot be cured, but it is very manageable. Symptoms and long-term risks respond well to lifestyle measures, hormonal contraception, insulin-sensitizing medication, and treatments aimed at the specific concern. Many features also ease after menopause, although the metabolic risks persist and need ongoing attention.

faqs.question: Can you get pregnant with PCOS?
faqs.answer: Yes. Most women with PCOS who want to conceive eventually can. PCOS causes irregular ovulation rather than absolute infertility. Weight loss can restore ovulation, and ovulation induction with letrozole as the first-line medication is highly effective. IVF is an option when first-line treatment does not work.

faqs.question: How is PCOS treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment is tailored to current priorities. Lifestyle measures are foundational for everyone. Combined hormonal contraception regulates cycles and reduces acne and excess hair for women not seeking pregnancy. Metformin helps with metabolic risk. Letrozole is first-line for fertility. Anti-androgens such as spironolactone treat hirsutism.

faqs.question: Does weight loss help PCOS?
faqs.answer: Yes. In women with overweight, losing just 5-10% of body weight can restore ovulation, lower androgen levels, improve insulin sensitivity, and ease symptoms. Weight loss is one of the most effective treatments, though it is not relevant for women with PCOS who are at a healthy weight.

faqs.question: Is there a specific diet for PCOS?
faqs.answer: No single diet has been proven best for PCOS. The 2023 international guideline recommends a sustainable, individualized eating pattern rather than a fad diet. Emphasizing vegetables, whole grains, lean protein, and lower-glycemic-index carbohydrates, with attention to portions, supports weight and insulin management. Consistency matters more than the specific plan.

faqs.question: Does PCOS increase the risk of diabetes?
faqs.answer: Yes. Women with PCOS have roughly a 4-fold higher risk of type 2 diabetes because insulin resistance is central to the syndrome. Guidelines recommend screening for impaired glucose tolerance at diagnosis and periodically afterward. The risk is substantially reduced by maintaining a healthy weight and treating insulin resistance.

faqs.question: What is the role of metformin in PCOS?
faqs.answer: Metformin is an insulin-sensitizing medication used in PCOS mainly for metabolic benefit, especially in women with impaired glucose tolerance or high diabetes risk. It also modestly improves menstrual regularity and supports weight management. It is started at a low dose and increased gradually to limit stomach side effects.

faqs.question: Why does PCOS cause excess hair growth?
faqs.answer: PCOS raises androgen levels, and androgens stimulate the growth of coarse, dark hair in a male pattern on the face, chest, and abdomen — a condition called hirsutism. The same hormones can cause acne and scalp hair thinning. Treatment combines hormonal contraception, anti-androgens, and cosmetic hair removal.

faqs.question: Can teenagers have PCOS?
faqs.answer: Yes, PCOS symptoms usually begin around puberty. Diagnosis in adolescents is more cautious, however, because irregular periods and multifollicular ovaries are common in normal early puberty. In teenagers, diagnosis relies on clear hyperandrogenism plus persistent menstrual irregularity, and ultrasound is not used within eight years of the first period.

faqs.question: What happens if PCOS is left untreated?
faqs.answer: Untreated PCOS raises the long-term risk of type 2 diabetes, high blood pressure, abnormal cholesterol, and cardiovascular disease. Years of absent periods expose the uterine lining to unopposed estrogen, increasing the risk of endometrial hyperplasia and cancer. Anxiety and depression are also more common. These risks are substantially reduced with management.

faqs.question: Why do women with PCOS need regular periods or progestin?
faqs.answer: When ovulation does not happen, the uterine lining is exposed to estrogen without the protective effect of progesterone, so it can thicken abnormally over time and raise endometrial cancer risk. Regular withdrawal bleeds from hormonal contraception, or periodic progestin courses, protect the lining and lower that risk.

faqs.question: Does PCOS go away after menopause?
faqs.answer: PCOS does not fully go away, but some features ease after menopause as androgen levels decline — periods are no longer an issue, and hirsutism and acne may improve. The metabolic and cardiovascular risks persist, however, so ongoing screening for diabetes, lipids, and blood pressure remains important after menopause.

faqs.question: How much does PCOS treatment cost?
faqs.answer: Costs vary by treatment. Lifestyle measures are low cost. Combined contraceptive pills and metformin are inexpensive generics widely available, including affordable generics in India. Fertility treatment, including ovulation induction monitoring and IVF, is more costly. Ongoing metabolic screening adds modest periodic expense.

faqs.question: Are the cysts in PCOS dangerous?
faqs.answer: The 'cysts' in PCOS are not true cysts and are not dangerous in themselves. They are small immature follicles that stopped developing because of the hormonal imbalance. They do not need to be removed and do not turn into cancer. The name is misleading, which is why some experts prefer different terminology.

faqs.question: Can PCOS cause mental health problems?
faqs.answer: Anxiety, depression, and disordered eating occur at substantially higher rates in women with PCOS than in the general population. The 2023 international guideline recommends screening for these conditions as a routine part of PCOS care. They are treatable, and addressing them is considered a core component of management.

faqs.question: What is the difference between PCOS and endometriosis?
faqs.answer: PCOS is a hormonal and metabolic disorder causing irregular ovulation, excess androgens, and metabolic risk. Endometriosis is a condition where tissue similar to the uterine lining grows outside the uterus, causing pelvic pain and painful periods. They are different diseases, although both can affect fertility and sometimes coexist.

faqs.question: When should I see a specialist for PCOS?
faqs.answer: See an endocrinologist or gynecologist if the diagnosis is uncertain, if symptoms are severe or rapidly worsening, if metabolic complications such as diabetes develop, or if standard treatment is not controlling symptoms. See a reproductive specialist for fertility if ovulation induction is needed, particularly if first-line treatment fails.

metaDescription: PCOS: a hormonal disorder causing irregular periods, excess hair, and insulin resistance. Symptoms, diagnosis, fertility options, and treatment explained.
